topicArea: Home, Practiced Space & What Displacement Actually Removes

questions.passage:
The last box goes into the van, and the rooms, still echoing with a decade of a family's life, already look like someone else's. Nothing legal has changed; the closing is next week. But something has plainly ended, and it did not end at the deed transfer and will not end at the handover of keys, which suggests that whatever a home is, it is not the house, and that the relation between the two is looser and stranger than the mortgage paperwork implies.

A house is shelter; a home is a practiced place. The practice is mostly invisible because it has succeeded: the hand that finds the light switch in the dark, the feet that skip the creaking stair without consulting the mind, the reach that knows where the glasses are before the eyes do. Home is stored in the body as much as in the building, a choreography rehearsed until it disappears, and this is why the first weeks anywhere new are so tiring; every act that was automatic has become a decision again. Home, on this account, is a verb performed until invisible, and the performance takes time that cannot be bought or skipped.

The temporal grain runs deep. A home accumulates in layers, the door frame with the penciled heights, the burn on the counter from the year everything went wrong, and it also projects forward, since making a home is an act of anticipation, a bet that there will be mornings here worth arranging for. Homesickness testifies to the same structure from exile: what aches is not the loss of shelter but the loss of the layered, anticipated place, and the famous disappointment of returns, the childhood house revisited and found small, records that the home remembered no longer exists, partly because the rememberer no longer does.

Every home also draws a line, and the line has two edges. Inward, it makes the backstage where the performed self can be set down, the room where no account of oneself is owed, which is among the deepest of ordinary goods. Outward, the same line excludes, and the condition of lacking a home shows what the line was holding: the person without one lacks not only roof but address, and without an address, bureaucratic personhood frays, no place to receive the summons, store the documents, be found; lacks a backstage, so that every hour is performed in public; lacks the place where one is unobserved by default. Homelessness, examined closely, is the subtraction of nearly everything this passage has described, which is why roof-counting always understates it.

The idea has its ideological uses, and honesty requires naming them. The idealization of domesticity historically assigned the making of home to women and then declined to call the making work, so that nostalgia for the warm hearth is often, unexamined, nostalgia for someone else's unpaid labor. Nor is the interior reliably tender; for some, the most dangerous room they enter is one inside their own address, and sentimentality about home has more than once supplied the curtain such rooms needed. The praise of home, like the praise of most beloved institutions, requires an audit of who paid for the warmth.

Against the rooted ideal stands a rival boast: the cosmopolitan at home everywhere, the digital nomad with a laptop and no fixed address, placelessness worn as sophistication. The boast deserves its own audit. Elective placelessness is underwritten by money, which makes anywhere briefly habitable, and it retains what displacement removes, the ability to stop, to return, to choose again. The refugee and the nomad may both lack an address, but one lacks it as a project and the other as a wound, and a philosophy of home that cannot tell them apart is not paying attention.

The van pulls away. What it carries is furniture; what it cannot carry is the fit, worn smooth by repetition, between a self and a setting, which will have to be rebuilt, gesture by gesture, in rooms that resist for a while and then, one unnoticed day, stop resisting. That labor is real, its loss is real, and a society reveals itself in how seriously it takes both: in what it does for those who must rebuild, and in whether it can tell the difference between people who left home and people whose home was taken.
questions.stem: Which of the following best expresses the main idea of the passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Home is a practiced, layered fit between self and setting rather than a structure, so its loss subtracts far more than shelter.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Because domestic idealization concealed unpaid labor and real domestic danger, the concept of home deserves retirement rather than any rehabilitation.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The disappointment of returning to a childhood house proves that home is an illusion of memory with no present reality.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Digital nomadism demonstrates that home has become obsolete for anyone with sufficient means and portable work.

explanations.A: Correct. The passage separates home from house through the body's invisible choreography and the temporal layering, shows via homelessness that the loss subtracts address, backstage, and default unobservedness, audits the idea's ideological uses without abandoning it, distinguishes elective from imposed placelessness, and closes on the reality of the rebuilding labor and what a society owes those who must perform it.
explanations.B: This choice converts the fifth paragraph's audit into a verdict of retirement. The passage names the concealed labor and the dangerous interiors, then continues to treat home's goods, the backstage, the fit worn smooth, as deep and real; the audit is a condition on the praise, not its cancellation.
explanations.C: This choice inflates the return's disappointment into a general unreality. The third paragraph explains the smallness precisely, the remembered home no longer exists partly because the rememberer no longer does, which presupposes homes exist in time, not that they are illusions.
explanations.D: This choice adopts the boast the sixth paragraph audits. Elective placelessness is underwritten by money and retains the ability to stop and choose again; far from obsolescence, the nomad case sharpens the definition by contrast with displacement's wound.

questions.stem: The primary function of the fifth paragraph is to:

choices.label: A
choices.text: argue that the documented dangers of domestic interiors ultimately outweigh the goods that homes provide their inhabitants.

choices.label: B
choices.text: explain why homesickness and the disappointment of returns share a single temporal structure.

choices.label: C
choices.text: introduce the contrast between elective and imposed placelessness developed in the following paragraph.

choices.label: D
choices.text: audit the idealization of home, naming the unpaid labor and concealed dangers that hearth sentimentality has served.

explanations.A: This choice converts an audit into a verdict of net harm. The paragraph names real costs, unwaged making, dangerous interiors, but weighs nothing against the backstage and the fit the passage elsewhere defends; the demand is honesty about who paid, not a conclusion that the goods lose.
explanations.B: This choice reaches back to the third paragraph's business. Homesickness and the shrunken childhood house belong to the temporal-grain discussion; the fifth paragraph has moved from home's structure to its ideology.
explanations.C: This choice hands the paragraph the sixth's assignment. The nomad and the refugee, elective versus imposed placelessness, arrive afterward; the fifth paragraph's subjects are domestic labor and the interior's dangers, not mobility.
explanations.D: Correct. The paragraph announces its office, the idea has its ideological uses, and honesty requires naming them, then performs the audit: domesticity assigned home-making to women and declined to call it work, nostalgia for the hearth is often nostalgia for unpaid labor, sentimentality has curtained dangerous rooms, and praise of home requires an audit of who paid for the warmth.

questions.stem: The description of home as a verb performed until invisible, in the second paragraph, most nearly means that:

choices.label: A
choices.text: families gradually stop noticing the architectural features of the houses they inhabit.

choices.label: B
choices.text: the work of homemaking is deliberately hidden from guests so that hospitality appears effortless.

choices.label: C
choices.text: homes must be actively maintained through cleaning and repair even when that ongoing labor goes entirely unacknowledged.

choices.label: D
choices.text: home is an ongoing practice of habituation whose success is its disappearance into automatic routine.

explanations.A: This choice locates the invisibility in the building rather than the practice. What disappears is not the house's features but the acts of inhabiting them, the switch found in the dark, the stair skipped without thought, a choreography absorbed into the body.
explanations.B: This choice imports an audience the paragraph does not stage. The invisibility is not concealment from guests but the self-erasure of successful habit; the performance is invisible to the performer, which is why new rooms exhaust by making every act a decision again.
explanations.C: This choice substitutes the fifth paragraph's labor politics for the second's phenomenology. Cleaning and repair are real work, and the passage later addresses unacknowledged labor, but the verb here is habituation, the rehearsal of gestures until they vanish, not maintenance.
explanations.D: Correct. The paragraph builds the reading step by step: the practice is mostly invisible because it has succeeded, home is stored in the body, a choreography rehearsed until it disappears, and new places tire us because the automatic becomes deliberate again. The verb's success is exactly its invisibility, a performance completed when no longer noticed.

questions.stem: The author's attitude toward the digital nomad's placelessness is best described as:

choices.label: A
choices.text: admiring, since the nomad has achieved the freedom from rootedness that most settled people only ever imagine.

choices.label: B
choices.text: auditing, granting the choice its reality while noting it is money-backed and retains what displacement removes.

choices.label: C
choices.text: hostile, treating elective wandering as a moral offense against those who cannot travel at all.

choices.label: D
choices.text: envious, conceding that placelessness is preferable to home for those able to sustain it indefinitely.

explanations.A: This choice accepts the boast at face value. The sixth paragraph's first move is to announce that the boast deserves its own audit, and the audit deflates it: anywhere is briefly habitable because money makes it so, which is not freedom from rootedness but a purchased substitute for it.
explanations.B: Correct. The paragraph parallels the fifth's method, the boast deserves its own audit, and delivers findings rather than a verdict of condemnation: elective placelessness is underwritten by money, and it retains what displacement removes, the ability to stop, to return, to choose again. The project-wound distinction closes the assessment: real choice, misdescribed as transcendence.
explanations.C: This choice sharpens analysis into accusation. No moral offense is charged against the nomad; the criticism falls on a philosophy of home that cannot tell the nomad's project from the refugee's wound, a failure of attention, not of ethics on the traveler's part.
explanations.D: This choice invents a preference ranking the passage never makes. Nothing concedes placelessness superior to home; the paragraph's whole labor is to show the nomad still possesses home's essential powers in portable form, which is why the boast overstates.

questions.stem: A city responds to homelessness by reporting the number of shelter beds available each night and treating unused beds as evidence the problem is solved. Based on the fourth paragraph, the author would most likely object that this approach:

choices.label: A
choices.text: fails only because shelter beds are typically located far from employment opportunities.

choices.label: B
choices.text: is sound in principle, since shelter is the one component of home that public policy can actually and reliably supply.

choices.label: C
choices.text: counts roofs while the actual subtraction includes address, backstage, and default unobservedness.

choices.label: D
choices.text: errs by ignoring the ideological history of domesticity that the fifth paragraph documents.

explanations.A: This choice narrows the objection to logistics. Location is never the fourth paragraph's complaint; the deficit it catalogs, no address for bureaucratic personhood, no backstage, no place unobserved by default, would survive beds placed next door to every workplace in the city.
explanations.B: This choice endorses the equation the paragraph exists to refuse. Shelter is what a house provides; the paragraph's inventory of what homelessness subtracts is precisely why roof-counting always understates it, so supplying roofs cannot in principle exhaust the policy question.
explanations.C: Correct. The fourth paragraph closes with the diagnosis this case triggers: homelessness is the subtraction of nearly everything the passage has described, which is why roof-counting always understates it. The person without a home lacks address, and with it bureaucratic personhood, lacks a backstage, and lacks the place of default unobservedness; a nightly bed, given and withdrawn by the shift, restores none of these.
explanations.D: This choice grabs the adjacent paragraph's critique. The unwaged-labor and dangerous-interior audit concerns the idealization of home, not the measurement of homelessness; the bed-counting error is a fourth-paragraph mistake about what was subtracted.

questions.stem: Which of the following, if true, would most weaken the second paragraph's claim that the at-home feeling is built by embodied repetition over time that cannot be bought or skipped?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Evidence that professional decorators can make new houses resemble their owners' previous homes in appearance.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Studies finding people moved into exact replicas of former dwellings reported full at-home automaticity immediately upon arrival.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Evidence that the first weeks in a new dwelling are noticeably less tiring for people who have moved many times before.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Surveys finding that most people describe feeling at home in their vehicles after years of daily commuting.

explanations.A: This choice changes the look, not the fit. Resemblance in appearance is surface; the claim concerns the body's rehearsed choreography, and a house that merely looks familiar still demands that every automatic act be relearned in new dimensions.
explanations.B: Correct. The claim holds that the fit is rehearsed into the body over unpurchasable time. Replica dwellings producing immediate automaticity, the dark-switch test passed on arrival, would show the choreography transferring with the layout rather than accruing through repetition in the place itself, letting the at-home state be effectively bought and skipped, which the claim says cannot happen.
explanations.C: This choice shows practice at the skill of moving, which the embodied account accommodates: serial movers may habituate faster, but faster is still a process over time, and the claim never fixed the rehearsal's duration, only its necessity.
explanations.D: This choice confirms the mechanism in another setting. Years of daily commuting is exactly the embodied repetition the paragraph describes; the vehicle becoming home-like through rehearsal supports the account rather than weakening it.

questions.stem: Which of the following findings would most strengthen the sixth paragraph's distinction between placelessness held as a project and placelessness suffered as a wound?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Evidence that digital nomads and refugees report similar difficulties obtaining reliable internet access abroad.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Evidence that many digital nomads eventually purchase permanent residences after several years of travel.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Studies finding wellbeing among the address-less tracked the retained ability to stop, return, and choose again, not mobility itself.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Surveys finding that refugees express far stronger attachment to their former homes than digital nomads express toward theirs.

explanations.A: This choice finds a shared logistical inconvenience, which the distinction predicts should be superficial: the paragraph's point is that identical surface conditions, no fixed address, conceal opposite situations, so a common complaint about connectivity neither tests nor supports the deeper division.
explanations.B: This choice shows nomads exercising the very ability the paragraph credits them with, choosing again, but by itself it is a biographical pattern; without any comparison to those who lack the ability, it cannot show that the retained option is what separates project from wound.
explanations.C: Correct. The distinction claims the operative variable is not the missing address but the retained powers, to stop, to return, to choose again, project versus wound. Wellbeing tracking exactly that variable, flourishing with the retained ability and deteriorating without it while mobility itself explains nothing, isolates the paragraph's proposed difference and confirms it does the work.
explanations.D: This choice measures attachment to former homes, a sentiment the distinction does not turn on; a nomad could love a childhood house intensely while placeless by project, and the wound consists in the stripping of options, not in the strength of retrospective feeling.
